name: Simon Willison
bio: Simon Willison is a British software developer, independent open-source contributor, and the co-creator of the Django web framework. Born in January 1981, he studied at the University of Bath and began his career in 2000 as a webmaster at Gameplay. In 2003 he joined the Lawrence Journal-World in Kansas on a year-long internship, where he and Adrian Holovaty created Django to streamline newsroom web development. After graduating in 2005 he joined Yahoo's Technology Development team, then in 2008 became a software architect at The Guardian. In 2010 he co-founded Lanyrd, a social conference directory backed by Y Combinator, which was acquired by Eventbrite in 2013 where he rose to Engineering Director. Since leaving Eventbrite he has worked full-time as an independent open-source developer building tools for data journalism. He created Datasette (2017), an open-source tool for exploring and publishing SQLite data; sqlite-utils, a CLI and Python library for manipulating SQLite databases; and LLM (2023), a command-line tool and Python library for accessing large language models. He coined the term 'prompt injection' in 2022 to describe a critical class of LLM security vulnerabilities. Willison is a Python Software Foundation board member (since 2022), a GitHub Star, and a participant in the GitHub Accelerator program (2023). He is based in Half Moon Bay, California.

timeline.event: Joined the Lawrence Journal-World; co-created Django web framework with Adrian Holovaty
Django publicly released as open source
Joined The Guardian as software architect, applying data journalism techniques
Co-founded Lanyrd, a social conference directory, with Natalie Downe
Lanyrd accepted into Y Combinator
Lanyrd acquired by Eventbrite; joined as engineering team member, later Engineering Director
Created and launched Datasette, an open-source tool for exploring and publishing SQLite data
Elected to the Python Software Foundation board
Coined the term 'prompt injection' to describe a fundamental class of LLM security vulnerabilities
Launched LLM, a CLI tool and Python library for accessing large language models via plugins
Participated in GitHub Accelerator program for Datasette
Guest on inaugural Pragmatic Engineer Podcast: AI tools for software engineers, without the hype
Appeared on Latent Space podcast: Things We Learned About LLMs in 2024
Guest on Real Python Podcast #236: Using LLMs for Python Development
Appeared on Accessibility and Gen AI Podcast discussing Datasette and AI tooling
Announced Datasette for Newsrooms, a product suite for investigative journalists, with Alex Garcia
Guest on Generationship podcast ep. #39: 'I Coined Prompt Injection'
Released LLM 0.26 with tool-use support for OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and Ollama models
Appeared on Half Stack Data Science podcast discussing LLMs for data journalism
Led NICAR 2026 workshop: Coding Agents for Data Analysis (3-hour session for data journalists)

key_contributions.title: Django
key_contributions.description: Co-created the Django web framework in 2003 at the Lawrence Journal-World. Django became one of the most popular Python web frameworks, powering Instagram, Pinterest, Mozilla, and thousands of other sites.

key_contributions.title: Datasette
key_contributions.description: Open-source multi-tool for exploring and publishing data from SQLite databases as interactive web applications. 10,800+ GitHub stars, with a plugin ecosystem of 100+ extensions.

key_contributions.title: LLM
key_contributions.description: Command-line tool and Python library for accessing large language models (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama) via a plugin architecture. 11,300+ GitHub stars.

key_contributions.title: sqlite-utils
key_contributions.description: Python CLI utility and library for manipulating SQLite databases with a clean, composable API. 2,000+ GitHub stars.

key_contributions.title: files-to-prompt
key_contributions.description: CLI tool to concatenate a directory of files into a single prompt for use with LLMs. 2,600+ GitHub stars.

key_contributions.title: shot-scraper
key_contributions.description: Command-line utility for taking automated screenshots of websites, powered by Playwright. 2,300+ GitHub stars.

key_contributions.title: Prompt Injection Research
key_contributions.description: Coined the term 'prompt injection' in September 2022 (named after SQL injection). Became a leading voice on LLM security, developing concepts like the 'lethal trifecta' for AI agent vulnerabilities.

key_contributions.title: Today I Learned (TIL)
key_contributions.description: Public collection of 575+ short-form learning notes, published as a Datasette-powered site. 1,400+ GitHub stars.

quotes.text: It's not about getting work done faster, it's about being able to ship projects that I wouldn't have been able to justify spending time on at all.
quotes.source: How I use LLMs to help me write code, Substack

quotes.text: Think of them as an over-confident pair programming assistant who's lightning fast at looking things up.
quotes.source: How I use LLMs to help me write code, Substack

quotes.text: If someone tells you that coding with LLMs is easy they are (probably unintentionally) misleading you.
quotes.source: How I use LLMs to help me write code, Substack

quotes.text: Most of the craft of getting good results out of an LLM comes down to managing its context.
quotes.source: How I use LLMs to help me write code, Substack

quotes.text: I think it's really useful to have a model hallucinate at you early because it helps you get that better mental model of what it can do.
The one thing you absolutely cannot outsource to the machine is testing that the code actually works.
quotes.source: How I use LLMs to help me write code, Substack
